skill.name: sca-trivy
skill.description: Run Trivy-based software composition analysis for containers, filesystems, dependencies, IaC files, secrets, licenses, and SBOM generation. The skill guides users through vulnerability triage, CI integration, and remediation prioritization.
skill.summary: Use Aqua Trivy to scan dependencies, containers, IaC, secrets, licenses, and SBOMs.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell-command examples, network URLs, filesystem paths, and high-risk keywords. Review found no prompt injection, malware intent, data exfiltration, or hidden execution; most high-risk hits are false positives from security terminology and documentation. The skill still warrants a medium warning because it instructs users to run Trivy, Docker, CI actions, filesystem scans, and credential-related commands.

medium_findings.title: User-Executed Security Scanning Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill contains numerous Trivy, Docker, CI, and helper-script commands. These are legitimate for an SCA skill, but they can pull images, inspect local files, create reports, and fail CI jobs when users run them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly documented and aligned with Trivy scanning workflows. Risk is operational rather than malicious because no hidden execution path or injected user input is present.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Scanning and Sensitive Report Generation
medium_findings.description: The skill directs users to scan local filesystems and generate JSON, SARIF, SBOM, and vulnerability reports. These outputs can expose package versions, internal architecture, and security findings if shared broadly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly warns that scan reports and SBOM files may be sensitive. The behavior is expected for SCA, but it creates confidentiality risk if outputs are mishandled.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling Examples Need Careful Use
medium_findings.description: The troubleshooting section shows registry authentication with environment variables and command-line username and password flags. These examples are common, but users should prefer credential helpers and secret managers to avoid exposing credentials in shell history or logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are clearly for legitimate registry authentication, but command-line and environment credential handling can leak secrets in some environments.

medium_findings.title: Networked Dependency and Image Data Sources
medium_findings.description: The skill references Trivy documentation, vulnerability databases, container registry workflows, and the Trivy vulnerability database repository. These sources are expected, but users should understand that scans may require network access and external database updates.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are legitimate public security references and Trivy database configuration. No suspicious endpoint or exfiltration pattern was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis labeled many Markdown code fences and inline command examples as Ruby backtick execution. The reviewed file is documentation, not Ruby code, so there is no automatic command execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited patterns are fenced Markdown examples and inline command references. No Ruby file or executable wrapper exists in the scanned skill file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: C2 Keyword Detection
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged C2-related keywords, but the cited lines contain SOC2 compliance metadata and text. There is no command-and-control behavior or remote control instruction in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations use SOC2 as a compliance framework reference. The context does not support a malware interpretation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keyword Detection
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography terms, but the cited lines are metadata and general product description. No weak cryptographic function, hashing implementation, or cryptographic configuration was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not implement or configure cryptography. The static hit appears to be caused by keyword matching in descriptive text.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hard Link Detection
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged hard link creation at a package-lock scan example. The line is a Trivy filesystem scan command, not a hard link operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact context is a Trivy command using a package manifest path. No hard link command or filesystem link creation is present.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Found
low_findings.description: The reviewed file does not contain instructions to override system messages, skip security analysis, claim pre-approval, or alter evaluator behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found no suspicious prompt override language. The file is primarily procedural security-scanning documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command-Line Credential Flags
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a command-line password example for private registry scanning. This should be documented as a last resort because process lists, logs, and shell history can expose secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible in the troubleshooting section and has a known operational secret-exposure risk. It is not malicious by itself.

content.user_title: Scan Dependencies and Containers with Trivy
content.value_statement: Teams need a repeatable way to find vulnerable packages, unsafe images, and IaC misconfigurations before release. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Trivy scans, SBOM output, CI gates, and remediation planning.
actual_capabilities: Guide Trivy scans for container images, filesystems, repositories, and IaC directories.
Prioritize CVE findings by severity, CVSS score, fix availability, and exploitability.
Generate SBOM guidance for CycloneDX and SPDX formats.
Prepare CI examples for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, and related pipelines.
Explain report handling for JSON, SARIF, vulnerability summaries, and license findings.
Document remediation workflows for dependency updates, base image changes, and false positive review.
limitations: Requires Trivy and any required container or registry tools to be installed by the user.
Does not replace human review of vulnerability exploitability or compensating controls.
Scan accuracy depends on current vulnerability databases, lock files, and image metadata.
Private registry scans require properly managed credentials and network access.

use_cases.title: Pre-Release Container Review
use_cases.description: Scan application images before release and produce prioritized remediation steps for critical and high findings.

use_cases.title: Dependency Risk Triage
use_cases.description: Analyze project manifests and lock files for known CVEs, fixed versions, and update recommendations.

use_cases.title: CI Security Gate Setup
use_cases.description: Add Trivy scanning to a pipeline with SARIF output, severity filters, and clear failure conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Basic Scan Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the sca-trivy skill to create a basic Trivy scan plan for this project. Include commands, expected outputs, and what I should review first.

prompt_templates.title: Triage Critical Findings
prompt_templates.scenario: Reviewing scan results after a Trivy run
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the sca-trivy skill to triage my Trivy findings. Prioritize critical and high issues by fix availability, exploitability, runtime exposure, and release impact.

prompt_templates.title: Add CI Pipeline Scanning
prompt_templates.scenario: Integrating security checks into delivery workflows
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the sca-trivy skill to design a CI security gate for this repository. Include scan type, severity thresholds, SARIF handling, artifacts, and failure rules.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Full SCA Program
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced vulnerability management and supply chain reporting
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the sca-trivy skill to define a full SCA workflow for containers, dependencies, IaC, secrets, licenses, SBOMs, baselines, exceptions, and executive reporting.

output_examples.input: Scan a production container image before deployment.
output: A scan plan covering image vulnerability checks, severity filters, and SBOM generation.
A triage summary that separates release blockers from accepted or informational findings.
A remediation list with package upgrades, base image updates, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: Add Trivy to a pull request workflow.
output: A CI design with filesystem scanning, SARIF upload, artifact retention, and failure thresholds.
Guidance for handling false positives through reviewed ignore entries.
A rollout plan that starts in reporting mode before enforcing release gates.

output_examples.input: Prepare SBOM and license reporting for an internal service.
output: An SBOM workflow using CycloneDX or SPDX formats for images and project files.
A license review approach that separates high, medium, and low license obligations.
Storage and access guidance for reports that may reveal internal architecture.

best_practices: Run scans from a trusted environment with current Trivy databases and reproducible image tags.
Treat Trivy reports, SBOMs, and SARIF files as sensitive security artifacts.
Document every ignored CVE with owner, reason, compensating controls, and review date.
anti_patterns: Do not paste registry passwords into commands when a credential helper or secret manager is available.
Do not fail builds on every untriaged finding without an agreed severity and exploitability policy.
Do not publish SBOMs or vulnerability reports without checking whether they expose internal details.

faq.question: Does this skill run Trivy automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflows and command guidance. The user chooses when and where to run commands.

faq.question: Can it scan private container images?
faq.answer: Yes, if the user has valid registry access and manages credentials securely.

faq.question: Does it support SBOM generation?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for CycloneDX and SPDX SBOM output from images and project files.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is written for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.

faq.question: Will it decide which vulnerabilities are exploitable?
faq.answer: It helps prioritize findings, but exploitability decisions still need project context and human review.

faq.question: Is it safe for regulated environments?
faq.answer: It can support regulated workflows, but teams must control report access, credential handling, and exception approvals.
